1: Chapter 21 marks a major structural and historical shift in the book of Jeremiah. This is the word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH, when King Zedekiah sent to him Pashhur the son of Malchiah and Zephaniah the priest, the son of Maaseiah, saying. The Zedekiah setting places this chapter in the period of the final Babylonian siege of Jerusalem (588–587 BC) — approximately 20 years after the Temple sermon of chapter 7. The delegation includes a Pashhur (different from the Pashhur of chapter 20 — this is son of Malchiah, not son of Immer) and Zephaniah the priest, who appears again in Jeremiah 29:25–26 and Jeremiah 37:3. The royal delegation signals that the crisis has reached the point where even the king who earlier ignored Jeremiah now turns to him.
2: Please inquire of YHWH for us, for Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is making war against us. Perhaps YHWH will deal with us according to all his wonderful deeds and will make him withdraw from us. Zedekiah's request is a classic covenant-consultation: inquire of YHWH (darash na ba'adenu et YHWH). The king appeals to historical precedent — his wonderful deeds (nifle'otav) — specifically, YHWH's past deliverances, perhaps including the deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib in 701 BC (2 Kings 19:35–36: the angel destroyed 185,000 Assyrians overnight). Zedekiah is hoping for a repeat Sennacherib-miracle: the besieging army supernaturally withdrawn. The hope is not irrational given precedent; it is the wrong hope in the wrong theological moment.
3: Then Jeremiah said to them: Thus you shall say to Zedekiah. The answer is addressed directly to the king. Jeremiah does not soften the message for the royal delegation; he delivers the same uncompromising word to king as to commoner. The answer will directly contradict what the king hoped to hear — no miraculous deliverance, no Sennacherib-repeat, but something far more theologically disorienting.
4: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Behold, I will turn back the weapons of war that are in your hands and with which you are fighting against the king of Babylon and against the Chaldeans who are besieging you outside the walls. And I will bring them together into the midst of this city. The divine oracle is a reversal of Zedekiah's hope. Rather than withdrawing the Babylonian army, YHWH will turn back the weapons of Jerusalem's defenders — disable them. Rather than keeping the Babylonians outside the walls, YHWH will bring them inside the city. The Sennacherib-miracle will not be repeated; instead the opposite will happen. The theological reason: Sennacherib attacked YHWH's city without YHWH's authorization; Nebuchadnezzar attacks with YHWH's commission.
5: I myself will fight against you with outstretched hand and strong arm, in anger and in fury and in great wrath. The most theologically shocking verse in the chapter: I myself will fight against you (nilchamti ani etkhem). YHWH fought for Israel against Egypt with outstretched hand and strong arm (Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 4:34; Jeremiah 26:8) — the Exodus-war formula now applied in reverse. YHWH is fighting against his own people with the same intensity with which he once fought for them. The covenantal framework makes this possible: the same God who saved Israel at the Exodus will judge them in the exile. The parallel use of Exodus language for judgment is one of Jeremiah's most powerful rhetorical moves.
6: I will strike down the inhabitants of this city, both man and beast. They shall die of a great pestilence. The pestilence component of the judgment triad is specified for the city's population — both human and animal. The inclusion of animals reflects the total ecological impact of siege and divine judgment (cf. the animal deaths in the drought oracle, Jeremiah 14:5–6). The besieged city will experience not only military attack but epidemic disease — a predictable result of the crowding, sanitation breakdown, and malnutrition of prolonged siege.
7: Afterward, declares YHWH, I will give Zedekiah king of Judah and his servants and the people in this city who survive the pestilence, sword, and famine into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and into the hand of their enemies, into the hand of those who seek their lives. He shall strike them down with the edge of the sword. He shall not pity them or spare them or have compassion. Zedekiah's personal fate is specified: he will be handed over to Nebuchadnezzar. The triple negation of divine compassion (will not pity, will not spare, will not have compassion) echoes Jeremiah 13:14 — the judgment has reached the point where the covenant mercy attributes are suspended. This was historically fulfilled in 2 Kings 25:6–7: Nebuchadnezzar executed Zedekiah's sons before his eyes and then blinded him.
8: And to this people you shall say: Thus says YHWH: Behold, I set before you the way of life and the way of death. The two-ways offer is one of Jeremiah's most striking pastoral interventions. The Deuteronomic framework of life and death (Deuteronomy 30:15–20: I set before you life and death, blessing and curse — choose life) is now recontextualized: the way of life is not covenant faithfulness leading to prosperity in the land, but surrender to Babylon. The theological inversion is complete: the natural instinct of covenant loyalty (resist the enemy) has become the way of death; surrender to the enemy has become the way of life.
9: Whoever stays in this city shall die by the sword, by famine, and by pestilence, but whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans who are besieging you shall live and shall have his life as a prize of war. The offer is concrete: those who remain in Jerusalem to fight will die by the judgment triad (sword, famine, pestilence). Those who go out and surrender (naphel el-hakasdim, fall/defect to the Chaldeans) will preserve their lives. The phrase life as a prize of war (nafshah lo leshalal) — life as spoil, as something snatched from the enemy — suggests that survival will be bare and undignified, but it will be survival. Jeremiah will repeat this offer throughout the siege period (Jeremiah 38:2).
10: For I have set my face against this city for harm and not for good, declares YHWH: it shall be given into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he shall burn it with fire. YHWH's face set against Jerusalem (rather than for it) is the ultimate reversal of the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:25–26: YHWH lift up his face to you). The Babylonian burning of Jerusalem is announced categorically: he shall burn it with fire. This is precisely what happened in 587 BC (2 Kings 25:9). The irreversibility of the flask-sign-act (Jeremiah 19) is here confirmed in explicit historical prediction.
11: And to the house of the king of Judah say, Hear the word of YHWH. A separate oracle addressed specifically to the royal house begins here (vv11–14), distinguishing the message to the Davidic dynasty from the message to the general population. The royal house still has a specific covenantal responsibility: to execute justice.
12: O house of David! Thus says YHWH: Execute justice in the morning, and deliver from the hand of the oppressor him who has been robbed, lest my wrath go forth like fire, and burn with none to quench it, because of your evil deeds. Execute justice in the morning (shiftu laboqer mishpat) — the king's primary covenantal obligation was judicial: to hear cases at the city gate in the morning (the customary time for judicial proceedings), to defend the oppressed and dispossess the oppressor. The royal failure in this regard (cf. Jeremiah 22:13–17 on Jehoiakim) is what has brought the judgment. Even now, in the siege, genuine judicial reform could theoretically alter the trajectory — but the people's response to the potter-oracle (Jeremiah 18:12: that is hopeless) suggests no such reform is forthcoming.
13: Behold, I am against you, O inhabitant of the valley, O rock of the plain, declares YHWH; you who say, Who shall come down against us, or who shall enter our dwellings? Jerusalem is addressed under geographical epithets: the inhabitant of the valley and rock of the plain describe the city's topographic setting — it sits on a ridge overlooking valleys, surrounded by heights. The city's rhetorical confidence in its natural defenses (who can come down against us?) is the geographic expression of Zion theology: the impregnable fortress that YHWH defends. Jeremiah announces that the very natural defenses the city trusts will be overcome.
14: I will punish you according to the fruit of your deeds, declares YHWH; I will kindle a fire in her forest, and it shall devour all that is around her. The judgment closes with the fire-in-the-forest image — the dense woodland that surrounded Jerusalem (the forests of the hill country) consumed by fire, extending the destruction beyond the city walls into the surrounding territory. According to the fruit of your deeds (kifri ma'aleleikhem): the covenant-recompense formula. The punishment is not arbitrary but the natural consequence of the covenant violations catalogued across the preceding chapters. The chapter ends with the irreversible verdict, and the loop of judgment-announced-judgment-executed closes on Jerusalem after 20 chapters of warning.
